summary: Beans fix atmospheric nitrogen that benefits heavy-feeding corn; corn stalks act as a trellis for climbing beans. Core of the Three Sisters; yield benefits are real but vary by variety and spacing.
Squash's sprawling leaves shade the soil, suppress weeds and retain moisture around corn.
Grown together in the Three Sisters; pairwise benefit is plausible (weed suppression, some N) but less directly studied than the full trio.
Popular pairing; basil may deter some pests and attract pollinators, but rigorous evidence for improved tomato yield or flavor is limited. Traditional, plausible.
Black walnut roots, hulls and leaves release juglone, a documented respiration inhibitor; tomatoes are highly sensitive (wilting, death). Keep tomatoes well outside the root zone.
Tagetes suppress root-knot nematodes via alpha-terthienyl; strongest when grown as a dense planting or rotation the prior season, not just a few interplanted flowers.
Widely repeated claim that alliums stunt legumes; little rigorous evidence. Folklore — shown only with the tradition toggle.
Nasturtium used as an aphid / squash-bug trap crop to draw pests off cucurbits; commonly recommended, limited controlled data.
White clover as orchard living mulch fixes nitrogen, retains moisture, suppresses weeds and feeds pollinators beneath apple.
Comfrey is a classic fruit-tree-guild chop-and-drop mulch; deep taproot is claimed to mine nutrients. Benefit plausible but 'dynamic accumulation' is not well quantified.
Native wild strawberry as edible living mulch under apple: suppresses weeds, holds moisture, feeds pollinators. Guild tradition.
Wild bergamot draws pollinators and predatory insects into the apple guild; pairwise benefit plausible, not quantified.
Purple coneflower pulls pollinators and beneficial insects toward the apple guild; traditional, unquantified.
Horseradish at the drip line is a classic fruit-tree-guild pest/disease deterrent claim; traditional, unproven.
Groundnut is an N-fixing native vine; scrambling at a persimmon's base can add nitrogen. Keep it off young trunks — it twines.
Wild strawberry groundcover beneath a pawpaw; both tolerate part shade. Guild tradition.
N-fixing groundnut alongside elderberry; both like moist ground. Plausible, untested pairwise.
Wild strawberry as living mulch under blueberry; pH needs overlap on the acidic side. Guild tradition.
Mulberry ripens with blueberries and lures birds away as a decoy crop; widely recommended, little controlled data.
Apple/crabapple are listed by extensions as juglone-susceptible; keep apple well outside a black walnut's root zone. Lists are observational.
Brambles (Rubus) are reported juglone-susceptible; separate from black walnut.
Blueberry is reported juglone-susceptible; keep it away from black walnut.
Both tall, aggressive heavy feeders; sunchoke is also allelopathic and crowds neighbors. Give them separate ground.
